task: I’m putting together a one-period digital literacy lesson pack for a middle-school class and want the pieces to feel like they belong together, not like I grabbed them randomly. Could you start on Slidesgo and pick one fun, classroom-appropriate presentation template that would work for a grade 7 or 8 lesson, ideally something bright and student-friendly rather than corporate, because I want to use that visual style as the theme for everything else? Open the actual template page and leave it open so I can see the preview images, and note whether it’s available for Google Slides or PowerPoint. Then use Google to find a printable worksheet or practice page for an 8th-grade student on basic marketing strategies or persuasion techniques, like identifying advertising tricks or persuasive techniques, and open the actual resource page so I can check that it really looks classroom-ready and printable. After that, go to Citation Machine and verify that Harvard style is actually available there by navigating to wherever the citation styles are shown or selectable, because I’ll need to cite the worksheet and any media correctly; leave that proof visible or keep the page open. Finally, on YouTube, find three beginner-friendly videos about online safety or cybersecurity basics that would make sense for students, open each video in its own tab so I can compare them, and play one of them briefly so you can tell me what the opening covers. At the end, send me a short lesson-pack summary with the Slidesgo template name and format option, the worksheet title and where it’s hosted, the evidence that Harvard style can be selected on the citation site, and the three YouTube video titles with their channel names.
grader_reasoning:
R1: success - The agent successfully navigated to the Slidesgo website and identified a template titled "Media Literacy and Digital Citizenship - 8th Grade". The agent confirmed that this template is available for both Google Slides and PowerPoint, which satisfies the requirement for a fun, classroom-appropriate template and an available download format. The template page was left open as requested.

R2: success - The agent successfully searched for and opened a printable worksheet on persuasive techniques. The resource, titled "Persuasive Techniques" (hosted on ReadWriteThink.org), was opened as a PDF in the browser, and the agent confirmed it had print/download controls, satisfying the requirement to find a printable worksheet and provide its title and hosting location.

R3: success - The agent navigated to `https://www.citationmachine.net/harvard` (Step 19). The snapshot of this page shows a heading "Harvard Citation Generator" and provides options to select source types (Website, Book, Journal), which confirms that Citation Machine supports Harvard style and provides the requested evidence.

R4: success - The agent successfully identified three YouTube videos related to online safety/cybersecurity basics. The titles and channel names provided in the summary ("Being Safe on the Internet" by AMAZE Org, "Safe Web Surfing: Top Tips for Kids and Teens Online" by watchwellcast, and "11 Internet Safety Tips for Your Online Security" by Kaspersky) match the video pages opened in the browser tabs (tabs 3, 4, and 5, as confirmed by the action history and snapshots).

R5: success - The agent successfully demonstrated browser-only proof by:
1. Leaving the Slidesgo template page open (Step 5).
2. Leaving the Citation Machine Harvard style page open (Step 19).
3. Opening three YouTube videos in separate tabs (Step 23/24/27/28).
4. Playing one of the YouTube videos briefly (Step 29, confirmed by `currentTime` change from 31.24 to 36.25).
5. Providing a description of the opening of the played video (Step 30/33), which covers the importance of online safety, limiting personal information, and privacy settings.
